---
title: "Rotation As Addition"
---

A rotation `rot(n,k)` moves a node by adding `k` in `C_n`.

Once a circle is an address system, motion becomes arithmetic. Starting from node `x`, a stride `k` sends the node to `x + k`, then the result is reduced modulo `n`.

Two rotations in a row do not create a new kind of motion. If the first stride is `b` and the second stride is `a`, the combined motion is the same as one stride of `a + b`. The widget lets you compare the two-step route with the one-step route.

<div class="warning-box">
The widget demonstrates the composition law as an executable reference. It is not a formal proof.
</div>

## Picture

<div class="dictionary-box" data-dictionary-id="CC-0101"></div>

<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="CC-T0001"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="CC-T0002"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="CC-T0003"></div>

<div class="widget-panel" data-widget="rotation_composition" data-theorem-id="CC-T0002" data-dictionary-ids="CC-0101"></div>

## Try It

Use `n = 12`, start `x = 8`, stride `b = 5`, and stride `a = 7`. Predict the final node before reading the widget output. Then change `a` and `b` while keeping `a + b` fixed; the final node should stay fixed.

::: {.checkpoint-box}

## Checkpoint

Why should `rot(n,a)(rot(n,b)(x))` and `rot(n,a+b)(x)` land on the same node?

:::

## Source Trail

<p data-paper-ref="papers/PAPER_01_FINITE_CIRCLES.md">Paper source: <a href="https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/blob/main/papers/PAPER_01_FINITE_CIRCLES.md">Circle Calculus I</a></p>

Python reference model:

```python
from circle_math.finite import Circle

C = Circle(12)
C.rot(C.rot(8, 5), 7) == C.rot(8, 12)
```

<script type="module" src="../../widgets/shared/widget_base.js"></script>
<script type="module" src="../../widgets/S1/rotation_composition.js"></script>
